final_answer:
Use one target percentage with a smooth incentive curve at launch, not a hard tier table.

A tier table sounds useful, but game-theory wise it creates cliffs. Validators will sit just above a tier, panic around price moves, and delegators will game the threshold. A smooth curve is better because every extra bit of `GILT` improves rewards gradually.

Recommended launch model:

```text
targetGiltShare = 10%
minGoldMultiplier = 60%
maxGoldMultiplier = 100%
```

Formula:

```text
gilt_share = GILT value / (GILT value + GOLD value)

gold_reward_multiplier =
minGoldMultiplier + (maxGoldMultiplier - minGoldMultiplier) * min(gilt_share / targetGiltShare, 1)
```

Example table from that formula:

```text
0% GILT share      => GOLD earns at 60% weight
2.5% GILT share    => GOLD earns at 70% weight
5% GILT share      => GOLD earns at 80% weight
7.5% GILT share    => GOLD earns at 90% weight
10%+ GILT share    => GOLD earns at 100% weight
```

So there is still an “incentive table” for users to understand, but the actual protocol rule is a smooth formula, not hard jumps.

Market effect:

```text
More GILT backing = better GOLD yield
Weak GILT backing = lower GOLD yield
No sudden validator failure from price movement
Delegators naturally prefer better-backed validators
Validators have a reason to hold more GILT
```

Why this matters: it creates demand for `GILT` without making staking fragile when prices move.

What should be done next: start with one configurable curve, then add governance parameters later if the market shows the curve is too weak or too aggressive.
